meta.description: Structured educational content for CyberResilient learning mode
meta.author: CyberResilient Project

glossary.MTTD: Mean Time to Detect — the average time between a security event occurring and it being discovered. Industry benchmark: < 24 hours. Top performers: < 1 hour.
glossary.MTTR: Mean Time to Respond — the average time from detection to containment. Industry benchmark: < 72 hours. Top performers: < 4 hours.
glossary.RTO: Recovery Time Objective — the maximum acceptable downtime for a system. Defined in the BIA (Business Impact Analysis) and validated through DR testing.
glossary.RPO: Recovery Point Objective — the maximum acceptable data loss measured in time. An RPO of 1 hour means you must have backups no older than 1 hour.
glossary.KPI: Key Performance Indicator — a measurable value that demonstrates how effectively an organization is achieving key objectives.
glossary.SIEM: Security Information and Event Management — a platform that collects, correlates, and analyzes security logs from across the organization.
glossary.EDR: Endpoint Detection and Response — software deployed on endpoints (laptops, servers) to detect and respond to threats in real-time.
glossary.NDR: Network Detection and Response — monitors network traffic for suspicious activity, lateral movement, and data exfiltration.
glossary.CASB: Cloud Access Security Broker — a security enforcement point between cloud service consumers and providers.
glossary.DLP: Data Loss Prevention — tools and policies that prevent unauthorized data transfers outside the organization.
glossary.PAM: Privileged Access Management — controls and monitors access for accounts with elevated permissions.
glossary.BIA: Business Impact Analysis — a process that identifies critical business functions and the impact of disruption.
glossary.RACI: Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed — a matrix that clarifies roles in processes and decisions.
glossary.IOC: Indicator of Compromise — forensic artifacts that indicate a potential intrusion (IP addresses, file hashes, domains).
glossary.CVE: Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — a standardized identifier for publicly known cybersecurity vulnerabilities.
glossary.CVSS: Common Vulnerability Scoring System — a framework for rating the severity of vulnerabilities on a 0-10 scale.
glossary.GRC: Governance, Risk, and Compliance — the integrated approach to managing organizational governance obligations, enterprise risk, and regulatory compliance.
glossary.SOC: Security Operations Center — the team and facility responsible for monitoring, detecting, and responding to security events 24/7.
glossary.NIST CSF: National Institute of Standards and Technology Cybersecurity Framework — a voluntary framework for managing cybersecurity risk, organized into six functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
glossary.ISO 27001: An international standard for information security management systems (ISMS). Certification requires an audit by an accredited body and demonstrates commitment to security.
glossary.NIST RMF: NIST Risk Management Framework (SP 800-37) — a structured process for managing security and privacy risk: Categorize → Select → Implement → Assess → Authorize → Monitor.
glossary.MITRE ATT&CK: Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge — a knowledge base of adversary behavior organized into 14 tactics and hundreds of techniques.
glossary.MFIPPA: Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act — Ontario legislation governing access to information and privacy protection for municipalities.
glossary.CYFSA: Child, Youth and Family Services Act — Ontario legislation with specific privacy protections for child welfare information.
glossary.CCM: Continuous Controls Monitoring — the practice of automatically and continuously testing the effectiveness of security controls rather than relying on periodic manual audits.
glossary.SOAR: Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response — platforms that automate incident response playbooks and integrate security tools.
glossary.SBOM: Software Bill of Materials — a formal record of all components and dependencies in a software application, essential for supply chain security.

overview.title: What is a Security Posture Dashboard?
overview.content: An executive security dashboard aggregates Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) like Mean Time to Detect (MTTD), Mean Time to Respond (MTTR), patch compliance, and vulnerability counts into a single view. CISOs use dashboards like this to brief leadership and board members on the organization's cyber risk profile. The metrics here map to NIST CSF functions and ISO 27001 controls.

kpi_deep_dive.title: Understanding Security KPIs
kpi_deep_dive.content: Each KPI tells a story about your security program's health. They aren't just numbers — they represent real capabilities that determine whether your organization can withstand an attack.

mttd.label: Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)
mttd.explanation: How quickly you discover breaches. The IBM Cost of Data Breach Report 2024 found organizations that detected breaches in under 200 days saved an average of $1.02M compared to those that took longer.
mttd.benchmark: Industry average: ~197 days. Best-in-class: < 24 hours. Your SIEM, EDR, and NDR tools directly impact this metric.
mttd.real_world: In the SolarWinds breach (2020), attackers had access for 14 months before detection. In the Target breach (2013), FireEye alerts were ignored for weeks. Detection speed is only as good as your response to alerts.

mttr.label: Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)
mttr.explanation: How quickly you contain and remediate after detection. This includes triage, investigation, containment, and initial remediation.
mttr.benchmark: Industry average: ~70 days. Best-in-class: < 4 hours. Automation (SOAR) and well-practiced IR playbooks are the biggest MTTR reducers.
mttr.real_world: During the Colonial Pipeline attack (2021), the company paid $4.4M ransom within hours because their MTTR for recovery was estimated at weeks. Organizations with rehearsed IR plans respond 2-3x faster.

patch_compliance.label: Patch Compliance Rate
patch_compliance.explanation: The percentage of systems with all critical patches applied within your defined SLA (typically 14-30 days for critical, 90 days for moderate).
patch_compliance.benchmark: Target: > 95% within SLA. Industry average: ~78%. NIST CSF PR.IP-12 and ISO 27001 A.8.8 both require patch management.
patch_compliance.real_world: The Equifax breach (2017) — 147 million records exposed because a known Apache Struts vulnerability (CVE-2017-5638) went unpatched for 2 months. The patch was available. One missed patch, $1.4 billion in costs.

vulnerabilities.label: Open Vulnerabilities
vulnerabilities.explanation: Count of unresolved vulnerabilities across your environment, typically categorized by CVSS score: Critical (9.0-10.0), High (7.0-8.9), Medium (4.0-6.9), Low (0.1-3.9).
vulnerabilities.benchmark: Focus on 'vulnerability aging' — how long vulns stay open. Critical: < 7 days. High: < 30 days. Medium: < 90 days. Zero criticals open > 30 days is a strong target.
vulnerabilities.real_world: The WannaCry ransomware (2017) exploited EternalBlue (MS17-010). Microsoft released the patch 2 months before the attack. Organizations that patched within 30 days were unaffected. Those that didn't: 200,000+ systems in 150 countries encrypted.

grc_connection.title: GRC Engineering: Dashboard as Evidence
grc_connection.content: In a modern GRC program, this dashboard isn't just for viewing — it's **compliance evidence**. Every metric displayed here maps to a specific control requirement. When an auditor asks 'Show me your vulnerability management process,' you don't open a spreadsheet — you show this dashboard with historical trend data, automated data collection, and traceable metrics. This is the shift from document-based GRC to **engineering-driven GRC**.

navigating_charts.title: Navigating the Dashboard Charts & Diagrams
navigating_charts.content: The executive dashboard uses several chart types to visualise your security posture at a glance. Here is how to read and navigate each one.

charts.name: Security Score Gauge
charts.description: A semi-circular gauge showing the organisation's overall security score (0–100), combining KPI performance across detection, response, patching, and compliance.
charts.how_to_read: The needle position shows the current score. The gauge has three colour zones: red (0–40) = critical, amber (40–70) = needs improvement, green (70–100) = healthy. A red threshold line at 60 marks the minimum acceptable baseline. The gold number at centre is your current score.
charts.what_to_look_for: If the score is below 60, your security programme has critical gaps. Review the KPI cards to the right of the gauge to identify which specific metrics are pulling the overall score down.

charts.name: KPI Metric Cards
charts.description: Eight key performance indicator cards showing MTTD, MTTR, patch compliance, vulnerability counts, and other operational metrics with trend deltas.
charts.how_to_read: Each card shows the metric name, current value, and a delta arrow. Green arrows (▲ or ▼ depending on context) indicate improvement; red arrows indicate deterioration. Metrics where 'lower is better' (e.g., MTTD, vulnerabilities) use inverse colouring — a decrease shows green.
charts.what_to_look_for: Red deltas on any card signal regression. Pay special attention to MTTD and MTTR — these directly measure your detection and response capability. Compare values against industry benchmarks shown in the KPI Deep Dive expandable sections.

charts.name: GRC Health Alert Strip
charts.description: Four coloured alert cards showing appetite breaches, expired risk evidence, stale NIST controls, and expiring policies. Only appears when issues exist.
charts.how_to_read: Each card shows a count with a contextual colour: green = zero issues, red/orange = items needing attention. The number in large text is the count of affected items. Subtext explains the threshold (e.g., 'Residual > 12' for appetite breaches).
charts.what_to_look_for: Any non-zero red card is a priority. Appetite breaches mean risks exceed your organisation's stated tolerance. Expired evidence means audit integrity is compromised. Click the 'Policies expiring soon' expander for details.

charts.name: Inherent vs Residual Risk Distribution
charts.description: A grouped bar chart comparing risk counts at each severity level (Very High, High, Medium, Low) before and after controls are applied.
charts.how_to_read: Solid bars = inherent risk (before controls). Faded bars = residual risk (after mitigation). Each severity level has two bars side-by-side. The gap between solid and faded bars shows how much your controls reduce risk at each level.
charts.what_to_look_for: If the Very High solid and faded bars are similar height, controls are not effectively reducing your highest risks. A large gap between inherent and residual bars at High/Very High levels shows strong control effectiveness.

charts.name: Risk Status Donut
charts.description: A donut chart showing the distribution of risks by lifecycle status: Open (red), Mitigating (orange), Monitoring (yellow), Accepted (blue), Closed (green).
charts.how_to_read: Each segment represents the proportion of risks in that status. A healthy register has mostly green (Closed) and blue (Accepted) segments, with a small proportion of red (Open).
charts.what_to_look_for: A large red (Open) segment means too many risks lack a treatment plan. A large orange (Mitigating) segment is healthy — it shows active remediation. If 'Accepted' is dominant, verify that risk acceptance decisions are properly documented and signed off.

charts.name: Monthly Security Incidents (12-Month Trend)
charts.description: A colour-gradient bar chart showing the count of security incidents per month over the past year.
charts.how_to_read: Each bar represents one month. Bar height = incident count. Colour shifts from dark green (low) through gold (moderate) to red (high). This shows both the absolute count and relative intensity at a glance.
charts.what_to_look_for: Look for upward trends (increasing bar heights) which signal worsening threat activity or detection improvements catching more events. Sudden spikes warrant investigation. A consistent downward trend after a policy change validates the intervention.

charts.name: Vulnerability Aging Distribution
charts.description: A donut chart showing how long open vulnerabilities have remained unpatched, grouped into age buckets.
charts.how_to_read: Each segment represents an age bucket (e.g., 0–7 days, 8–30 days, 31–90 days, 90+ days). Green = recent (being addressed), red = aged (neglected). The hole in the centre is decorative.
charts.what_to_look_for: Large red/orange segments mean vulnerabilities are aging beyond your patching SLA. If the '90+ days' segment is growing, your vulnerability management process needs attention. Critical vulnerabilities should never appear in the 30+ day buckets.

charts.name: Compliance Trend (NIST CSF & ISO 27001)
charts.description: A dual-line chart tracking NIST CSF and ISO 27001 compliance percentages over quarterly intervals.
charts.how_to_read: The gold line tracks NIST CSF compliance; the green line tracks ISO 27001. Both use a 0–100% Y-axis. Points are connected with markers showing the exact value at each quarter. Hover for precise percentages.
charts.what_to_look_for: Both lines should trend upward over time. If one framework's line drops while the other rises, investigate which controls changed. Divergence between the two lines may indicate gaps in framework mapping. Target: both lines above 80%.

charts.name: Threat Categories
charts.description: A horizontal bar chart showing the count of incidents by threat category (e.g., Malware, Phishing, Unauthorised Access), colour-coded by severity.
charts.how_to_read: Each bar represents a threat category. Bar length = number of incidents. Colours indicate severity: red = Critical, orange = High, gold = Medium, green = Low. Categories are listed vertically for easy comparison.
charts.what_to_look_for: The longest bars reveal your top threat vectors. If 'Phishing' dominates, invest in awareness training and email gateway controls. If 'Malware' is highest, focus on endpoint protection and patch compliance.

charts.name: Department Risk Comparison (Radar Chart)
charts.description: A spider/radar chart comparing all departments across five dimensions: Risk Score, Compliance, Patch Compliance, Incident Score, and Training. Only visible when 'All Departments' is selected.
charts.how_to_read: Each coloured polygon represents one department. The five axes extend from the centre (0) to the outer edge (100). The further a polygon reaches on an axis, the higher that department scores on that dimension. An ideal department would fill the entire pentagon to 100 on all axes.
charts.what_to_look_for: Dips inward on any axis reveal departmental weaknesses. Compare polygon shapes: departments with similar shapes have similar risk profiles. If most departments dip on the same axis (e.g., Training), it signals an organisation-wide gap. Use the department dropdown to filter the table and remove the radar to focus on one department.

charts.name: Department Metrics Table
charts.description: A styled data table with conditional colour gradients showing each department's Risk Score, Compliance %, Open Incidents, and Patch Compliance %.
charts.how_to_read: Columns use colour gradients to highlight relative performance: Risk Score uses red-yellow-green (reversed — lower is better), while Compliance % and Patch Compliance % use green-yellow-red (higher is better). This lets you instantly spot the best and worst performers.
charts.what_to_look_for: Red-shaded cells are immediate attention items. Compare departments to identify outliers. A department with high Risk Score AND low Compliance % is your highest-priority remediation target.

overview.title: What is DR/BC Testing?
overview.content: Disaster Recovery (DR) and Business Continuity (BC) testing validates that your organization can recover critical systems within target timeframes. **RTO** (Recovery Time Objective) = max acceptable downtime. **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective) = max acceptable data loss. Organizations typically test quarterly for Tier 1 systems per NIST SP 800-34.

cases.name: Maersk / NotPetya (2017)
cases.summary: The shipping giant lost its entire IT infrastructure — 49,000 laptops, 4,000 servers, across 130 countries — in 7 minutes. Recovery took 10 days. A single domain controller in Ghana that was offline during the attack saved the company. Without it, total rebuild would have taken months.
cases.lesson: DR testing must include scenarios where Active Directory is completely destroyed. Offline backups and geographic diversity are critical. Maersk estimated $300M in losses.
cases.rto_context: Maersk's actual recovery: 10 days for core IT. Their RTO target was likely < 24 hours for Tier 1. The gap was catastrophic.

cases.name: Colonial Pipeline (2021)
cases.summary: A ransomware attack (DarkSide group) shut down the largest fuel pipeline in the US. Colonial paid $4.4M ransom because their estimated recovery time without the decryption key was weeks. The FBI later recovered $2.3M of the ransom.
cases.lesson: The decision to pay ransom was driven by inadequate DR capability. If Colonial could have recovered within hours from immutable backups, they would never have paid. DR readiness directly reduces ransomware leverage.
cases.rto_context: Run the ransomware scenario here and compare your simulated RTO to Colonial's 5+ day outage. If your RTO is longer than your tolerance for ransom payment, your DR plan needs work.

cases.name: City of Atlanta (2018)
cases.summary: SamSam ransomware encrypted city systems. Atlanta refused to pay the $51,000 ransom. Recovery cost: $17 million. Some court records were permanently lost because backups were not properly maintained.
cases.lesson: The cost of recovery without preparation far exceeds ransom demands AND the cost of proper DR infrastructure. RPO failures (lost court records) can be permanent. Test your backup integrity — having backups isn't enough if they're corrupted or incomplete.
cases.rto_context: Atlanta's recovery took months for full restoration. Municipal governments handle life-safety services — this isn't just about money, it's about public safety.

try_this.title: Try This Exercise
exercises: Select the 'ERP/Financial System' and run the Ransomware scenario. Compare the estimated RTO to Colonial Pipeline's 5-day outage. Could your finance team operate manually for that long?
Run every scenario against your Tier 1 systems. If any simulation FAILS (red), that's a real gap that would hurt during a real incident. What would you change about the DR strategy to make it pass?
Look at the RACI matrix. Is there a single person who is 'Responsible' for too many activities? That's a bottleneck. In a real crisis, that person becomes a single point of failure.

grc_connection.title: GRC Engineering: DR Testing as Compliance Evidence
grc_connection.content: Every simulation you run here is logged with timestamps, parameters, and results. This simulation history serves as **audit evidence** for NIST CSF PR.IP-9 (Recovery plans), ISO 27001 A.5.29 (ICT readiness for business continuity), and SOC 2 A1.2 (Recovery objectives). Instead of documenting 'we test DR quarterly' in a spreadsheet, auditors can see actual test results with pass/fail status, trend data, and gap analysis. This is what compliance engineering looks like — evidence is generated as a byproduct of doing the work, not as a separate documentation exercise.

navigating_charts.title: Navigating the DR Simulator Charts & Diagrams
navigating_charts.content: The DR Simulator page shows simulation results using gauges, tables, and history records. Here is how to read each element.

charts.name: RTO Gauge (Recovery Time Objective)
charts.description: A gauge showing the estimated recovery time in hours compared to the system's RTO target. Only appears after running a simulation.
charts.how_to_read: The needle shows the estimated RTO in hours. A gold threshold line marks the target RTO. The gauge colour changes based on pass/fail: green if the estimate is within the target, red if it exceeds it. The delta value below the number shows the gap (positive = exceeded target, negative = within target).
charts.what_to_look_for: A red gauge means the system cannot recover within its target timeframe under this scenario. Compare the 'Estimated' value to the 'Target' value shown below the gauge. Large gaps indicate your DR strategy needs improvement — consider faster backup solutions or reduced dependencies.

charts.name: RPO Gauge (Recovery Point Objective)
charts.description: A gauge showing the estimated data loss window in hours compared to the system's RPO target. Only appears after running a simulation.
charts.how_to_read: Works identically to the RTO gauge but measures data loss. The needle shows estimated hours of data that could be lost. Green = within target RPO, red = exceeds it. The delta shows how far above or below the target you are.
charts.what_to_look_for: A red RPO gauge means your backup frequency is insufficient for this scenario. If estimated RPO is 8 hours but target is 1 hour, you need more frequent backups, real-time replication, or a different DR strategy for this system tier.

charts.name: RACI Matrix Table
charts.description: A table showing who is Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed for each DR activation activity.
charts.how_to_read: Each row is an activity (e.g., 'Activate DR Plan', 'Restore from Backup'). The four columns assign roles: R = does the work, A = owns the decision, C = provides input, I = kept informed. Every activity must have exactly one A (accountable person).
charts.what_to_look_for: Look for bottlenecks — if one person appears as 'R' in too many rows, they become a single point of failure during a real crisis. Also verify that critical activities have backup personnel identified.

charts.name: Simulation History Table
charts.description: A chronological record of all DR simulations run, showing system, scenario, severity, targets vs actuals, pass/fail result, and which user ran the test.
charts.how_to_read: Each row is one simulation run. Green ✅ PASS means both RTO and RPO targets were met. Red ❌ FAIL means at least one was exceeded. Compare Target vs Actual columns to see the gap.
charts.what_to_look_for: Look for patterns: does a specific system consistently fail? Does a particular scenario always exceed targets? Recurring failures for the same system indicate a structural DR weakness that needs investment, not just re-testing.

overview.title: What is Incident Response?
overview.content: Incident Response (IR) is the systematic process of preparing for, detecting, containing, eradicating, and recovering from cybersecurity incidents. NIST SP 800-61 Rev 2 defines four phases: **Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Post-Incident Activity**. Every organization needs a tested IR plan — tabletop exercises simulate incidents so teams can practice decision-making under pressure.

cases.name: SolarWinds Supply Chain Attack (2020)
cases.summary: Russian state actors (APT29/Cozy Bear) compromised SolarWinds' Orion build process, inserting the SUNBURST backdoor into updates distributed to 18,000 organizations. FireEye discovered the breach when investigating a stolen red team toolkit.
cases.ir_timeline: The attackers had access from March 2020. Not detected until December 2020 — 9 months of dwell time. Once FireEye disclosed, affected organizations had to execute all IR phases simultaneously across their entire environment.
cases.lesson: This is why Preparation (Phase 1) matters most. Organizations with mature IR programs, pre-staged forensic tools, and practiced supply chain compromise playbooks responded in days. Others took months.

cases.name: Capital One Data Breach (2019)
cases.summary: A former AWS employee exploited a misconfigured WAF to access 106 million customer records via SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) against EC2 metadata endpoints. The breach was reported to Capital One via a responsible disclosure email.
cases.ir_timeline: The breach occurred March-July 2019. Capital One was notified July 17 via email. Within 2 days, they had contained, notified law enforcement, and begun customer notification. Fast response despite the severity.
cases.lesson: Capital One's Detection & Analysis phase relied on an external report — their internal detection missed it. But their Containment-to-Recovery execution was textbook fast. Practice both: improve detection AND response speed.

tabletop_guide.title: How to Run Effective Tabletop Exercises
tabletop_guide.content: Tabletop exercises are discussion-based simulations where team members walk through a scenario and describe how they would respond. They test your IR plan, identify gaps in procedures, and build decision-making muscle memory.
tips: Include non-technical participants (legal, HR, communications, executives) — real incidents require cross-functional coordination
Use realistic scenarios based on actual threat intelligence for your industry
Inject complications mid-exercise: 'The backup server was also encrypted' or 'A reporter just called the front desk'
Focus on decision-making and communication, not technical minutiae
Always conduct a hot wash immediately after — what surprised people? what was unclear?
Schedule quarterly for critical scenarios, annually for comprehensive exercises

grc_connection.title: GRC Engineering: IR as a Compliance Requirement
grc_connection.content: A tested IR plan isn't optional — it's required by virtually every compliance framework. NIST CSF RS.RP-1 requires an IR plan that is 'executed during or after an incident.' ISO 27001 A.5.24-A.5.28 covers the entire IR lifecycle. SOC 2 CC7.3-CC7.5 requires incident detection, response, and communication. The key word is **tested** — having a document is insufficient. Auditors want evidence of tabletop exercises, after-action reports, and plan updates. Every exercise you complete here generates that evidence. The post-mortem form creates a structured lessons-learned document aligned with NIST 800-61r2 Section 3.4. This is compliance engineering: the tool does the documentation for you.

navigating_charts.title: Navigating the Incident Response Charts & Diagrams
navigating_charts.content: The IR Center uses a visual flow chart and interactive tabletop exercises. Here is how to read the key visual element.

charts.name: IR Lifecycle Flow Chart
charts.description: A horizontal flow diagram showing the six NIST SP 800-61r2 incident response phases connected by arrows: Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned.
charts.how_to_read: Each phase is represented by a coloured circle with an icon. Arrows (→) connect the phases left-to-right showing the standard sequence. Colours are thematic: green (Preparation), blue (Detection), orange (Containment), red (Eradication), purple (Recovery), gold (Lessons Learned). Click each phase's expander above the flow chart for detailed activities.
charts.what_to_look_for: Use this as a checklist during an actual incident — work through each phase sequentially. The expanders above contain specific activities for each phase including organisation-specific context. The flow is linear but in practice you may loop back from Eradication to Detection if new IOCs are found.

charts.name: Tabletop Exercise Scorecards
charts.description: Interactive decision-based exercises where each answer is scored and colour-coded feedback is provided. A running score tracks performance across all questions.
charts.how_to_read: Select an exercise scenario, then answer each question by choosing one of the response options. After each answer, feedback appears: green ✅ = correct (10 points), amber ⚠️ = partial (3–5 points), red ❌ = incorrect (0 points). Your total score is tallied at the end.
charts.what_to_look_for: Focus on the feedback text, not just the score. Partial answers often reveal nuances — for example, the difference between 'notify immediately' vs 'wait for confirmation' under MFIPPA breach obligations. Use incorrect answers as learning opportunities to update your IR playbooks.

overview.title: What is a Risk Register?
overview.content: A risk register is the central tool for tracking identified cybersecurity risks. Each risk is scored using a **Likelihood × Impact** matrix (typically 5×5), producing a risk score from 1–25. Risks are categorized as Low (1–4), Medium (5–9), High (10–15), or Very High (16–25). Risk owners are assigned to drive mitigation actions. This process aligns with ISO 31000 and NIST RMF (SP 800-37).

heat_map_guide.title: Reading the Risk Heat Map
heat_map_guide.content: The 5×5 heat map plots risks by Likelihood (vertical) and Impact (horizontal). Colors represent severity: green (Low), yellow (Medium), orange (High), red (Very High). The goal isn't zero risks — it's understanding which risks are above your **risk appetite** (the level of risk the organization is willing to accept) and driving those down through controls.
key_concepts: **Risk Appetite vs. Risk Tolerance**: Appetite is the broad level of risk you're willing to accept. Tolerance is the acceptable variation around specific objectives. A 'Very High' risk with no mitigation plan violates both.

**Inherent vs. Residual Risk**: Inherent = risk before controls. Residual = risk after controls. Your risk register should track both. If a risk is inherently 'Very High' (5×5=25) but mitigated to 'Medium' (3×2=6), that shows your controls are working.

**Risk Treatment Options**: Accept (within appetite), Mitigate (reduce likelihood/impact), Transfer (insurance/outsource), Avoid (eliminate the activity). Every risk needs a documented treatment decision.

cases.name: Capital One WAF Misconfiguration
cases.how_it_scores: Category: Cloud Security. Likelihood: 4 (Likely — SSRF is a well-known technique, and misconfigurations are common). Impact: 5 (Catastrophic — 106M records). Score: 20 (Very High). This should have been their #1 risk item with immediate mitigation.
cases.mitigation: WAF rule review, SSRF protection on EC2 metadata, IMDSv2 enforcement, cloud security posture management (CSPM) tools.
cases.lesson: A single misconfigured WAF rule exposed 106 million records. Risk registers must include cloud-specific risks scored at their true severity, and CSPM tools should continuously validate configurations.

cases.name: Unpatched VPN Appliance
cases.how_it_scores: Category: Vulnerability Management. Likelihood: 5 (Almost Certain — VPN vulns are actively exploited by nation-states). Impact: 4 (Major — provides initial access to the network). Score: 20 (Very High). This is how Pulse Secure VPN vulns (CVE-2021-22893) were exploited by APT groups.
cases.mitigation: Emergency patching within 48 hours, network segmentation, MFA on VPN, monitoring VPN logs for anomalous access patterns.
cases.lesson: Nation-state actors actively exploit unpatched VPN appliances within days of disclosure. Very High risks like these demand emergency patching SLAs and compensating controls until patches are applied.

try_this.title: Try This Exercise
exercises: Create a new risk for 'Unpatched VPN Gateway' — set Likelihood to 5, Impact to 4. This gives a score of 20 (Very High). What mitigation would you write? Think about both immediate actions (patch) and long-term improvements (automated patching).

Look at the Architecture Advisor tab. Run an assessment for a cloud SaaS product your organization uses. Every failed check represents an unmitigated risk that should appear in the risk register. Try creating those risks.
Find the highest-scoring risk in the register. What would it take to move it from 'Open' to 'Mitigating'? What specific controls would reduce the likelihood or impact?

grc_connection.title: GRC Engineering: The Risk Register as a Living System
grc_connection.content:
In traditional GRC, risk registers live in Excel spreadsheets. They get updated before audits, forgotten between audits, and become permanently out of date. Engineering-driven GRC treats the risk register as a **living system** with real-time data.

Here, every risk change is audit-logged: who created it, who changed the score, when mitigation status changed. This creates an **auditable chain of custody** for risk decisions. When an auditor asks 'How do you manage risk?', you show them the risk register, the audit trail showing regular reviews, and the evidence that scores change as controls are implemented.

This maps directly to: NIST CSF ID.RA (Risk Assessment), ISO 31000 Clause 6 (Risk Assessment Process), and ISO 27001 A.5.1 (Policies for information security). The architecture advisor in Tab 2 operationalizes NIST CSF ID.SC (Supply Chain Risk Management) and ISO 27001 A.5.19-A.5.22 (Supplier relationships).

navigating_charts.title: Navigating the Risk Register Charts & Diagrams
navigating_charts.content: The Risk Register uses heat maps, grouped bar charts, and detailed expanders to visualise your organisation's risk posture. Here is how to read each one.

charts.name: 5×5 Risk Heat Map
charts.description: A colour-gradient matrix plotting Likelihood (Y-axis, 1–5) against Impact (X-axis, 1–5). Each cell shows the risk score (Likelihood × Impact) and the count of risks in that cell.
charts.how_to_read: Cell colour ranges from dark green (low scores, bottom-left) through yellow and orange to dark red (high scores, top-right). Numbers in each cell show the score and, in parentheses, how many risks fall in that cell. A dashed red border marks the risk appetite boundary — any risk above this threshold requires executive sign-off before closure. Use the radio toggle to switch between Inherent (before controls) and Residual (after controls) views.
charts.what_to_look_for: Clusters of risks in the top-right red zone are your highest priorities. Compare Inherent vs Residual views — if risks move from red cells to green cells, your controls are working. Risks that remain in the red zone after switching to Residual view need stronger mitigation or risk acceptance sign-off.

charts.name: Inherent vs Residual Distribution (Grouped Bar Chart)
charts.description: A grouped bar chart comparing the count of risks at each severity level (Very High, High, Medium, Low) before and after controls.
charts.how_to_read: Two bars per severity level: solid bars = inherent risk count, faded bars = residual risk count. Bar colours match severity: dark red (Very High), red (High), orange (Medium), green (Low). The gap between solid and faded bars represents your control effectiveness.
charts.what_to_look_for: The goal is for faded (residual) bars to be shorter than solid (inherent) bars, especially at Very High and High levels. If both bars are similar height at a severity level, controls are not adequately reducing risk. A well-managed register shows inherent risks shifting from Very High/High to Medium/Low in the residual view.

charts.name: Risk Detail Expanders
charts.description: Expandable cards for each risk showing inherent and residual scores as colour-coded badges, mitigation details, evidence dates, and lifecycle status.
charts.how_to_read: Each risk card has a colour-coded header showing the inherent score badge (e.g., 'Very High (20)'). Inside, you see inherent vs residual score comparison, mitigation strategy, effectiveness rating, evidence date, and status lifecycle. Risks exceeding the appetite threshold show a red warning requiring sign-off before closure.
charts.what_to_look_for: Look for risks with expired evidence (flagged with 🗂️) — these need evidence refresh. Risks in 'Open' status with Very High inherent scores are your top priorities. The 'Appetite Breach' warning means the residual score still exceeds your organisation's stated risk tolerance.

overview.title: Why Compliance Frameworks Matter
overview.content: Compliance frameworks like **NIST CSF** and **ISO 27001** provide structured approaches to managing cybersecurity risk. They are not checkbox exercises — they are engineering blueprints for building a defensible security program.

grc_engineering.title: The Compliance Engineering Manifesto
grc_engineering.content: Traditional GRC relies on spreadsheets, manual evidence collection, and periodic audits. Compliance engineering treats compliance as a continuous, automated, and integrated part of operations. Here's the difference:

comparison.traditional: Annual risk assessments documented in Excel
comparison.engineering: Continuous risk scoring with live data feeds from vulnerability scanners, threat intel, and asset inventory

comparison.traditional: Evidence collection as a fire drill before audits
comparison.engineering: Evidence generated automatically as a byproduct of security operations (logs, scan reports, training records)
Continuous Controls Monitoring (CCM) with real-time dashboards and automated alerts on control failures

comparison.traditional: Policies reviewed annually, stored in SharePoint
comparison.engineering: Policy-as-Code: policies mapped to controls, controls mapped to evidence, with automated policy lifecycle tracking

comparison.traditional: Risk register updated before board meetings
comparison.engineering: Risk register updated in real-time with audit trail, automated scoring, and integration into operational workflows

comparison.traditional: Audit prep takes 2-3 months of scrambling
comparison.engineering: Audit-ready 365 days a year because evidence is continuously collected and organized

evidence_collection.title: Evidence Collection: From Spreadsheets to Automation
evidence_collection.content: Every compliance framework requires evidence that controls are implemented AND operating effectively. The gap between 'implemented' and 'operating effectively' is where most organizations fail audits.

evidence_types.description: Automated outputs from security tools — vulnerability scan reports, SIEM alert summaries, patch compliance dashboards, access reviews.
evidence_types.example: This dashboard's KPI data is technical evidence for NIST CSF DE.CM (Security Continuous Monitoring). Export it, and you've just collected evidence for 5 controls.

evidence_types.description: Records that processes are being followed — DR test results, tabletop exercise reports, change management tickets, risk review minutes.
evidence_types.example: Every DR simulation logged in the history table is process evidence for ISO 27001 A.5.29 (ICT readiness for BC). The simulation result, the timestamp, the user who ran it — all audit evidence.

evidence_types.description: Proof of management oversight — approved policies, risk acceptance decisions, audit committee minutes, security awareness completion rates.
evidence_types.example: The policy lifecycle tracker shows policy approval dates, review cycles, and expiration. This is governance evidence for ISO 27001 A.5.1 (Policies for information security).

evidence_types.description: Tamper-evident logs of who did what, when — user access logs, configuration change records, risk register modification history.
evidence_types.example: The Audit Log page shows every risk create/update/delete with username and timestamp. This is evidence for NIST 800-53 AU-2 (Event Logging) and ISO 27001 A.8.15 (Logging).

nist_csf_deep_dive.title: NIST CSF 2.0: The Six Functions Explained

Govern.purpose: Establish and monitor the organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, expectations, and policy. NEW in CSF 2.0 — elevates governance as a cross-cutting function.
Govern.real_world: This is where the C-suite meets cybersecurity. Governance isn't about firewalls — it's about policies, roles, risk appetite, and accountability. Who decides what level of risk is acceptable? That's GOVERN.
Govern.key_categories: GV.OC (Organizational Context), GV.RM (Risk Management Strategy), GV.RR (Roles, Responsibilities)
Govern.grc_tip: Map your policy library to GOVERN categories. Each approved policy is evidence. Each policy review triggers an audit log entry. Evidence flows automatically.

Identify.purpose: Understand your organization's cybersecurity risk posture: assets, vulnerabilities, threats, and risk exposure.
Identify.real_world: You can't protect what you don't know about. The Target breach (2013) partly succeeded because their asset inventory didn't adequately track vendor network connections. Shadow IT and unmanaged assets are IDENTIFY failures.
Identify.grc_tip: Your risk register IS the ID.RA evidence. Asset inventory feeds ID.AM. The architecture advisor supports ID.SC (Supply Chain Risk Management). All interconnected.

Protect.purpose: Apply safeguards to manage and reduce cybersecurity risk.
Protect.real_world: Protection controls include access management, encryption, security awareness training, and data security. The principle of least privilege is a PROTECT control — if Capital One's IAM roles had been scoped correctly, the SSRF wouldn't have accessed customer data.
Protect.key_categories: PR.AA (Identity Management & Access Control), PR.DS (Data Security), PR.AT (Awareness & Training)
Protect.grc_tip: Access reviews, encryption at rest/in transit, training completion rates — all are PROTECT evidence. Automate collection from IAM, DLP, and LMS systems.

Detect.purpose: Enable timely discovery and analysis of cybersecurity incidents and anomalies.
Detect.real_world: MTTD is your DETECT metric. SolarWinds went undetected for 9 months. Organizations with mature DETECT capabilities (tuned SIEM rules, behavioral analytics, threat hunting) would have caught anomalous build system changes.
Detect.grc_tip: SIEM alert metrics, detection rule coverage (map to MITRE ATT&CK), and false positive rates are DETECT evidence. Your Threat Intel page shows ATT&CK coverage — that's DE.AE evidence.

Respond.purpose: Take action when cybersecurity incidents are detected.
Respond.real_world: MTTR is your RESPOND metric. Colonial Pipeline's response included paying ransom — a RESPOND failure. Atlanta's response (refusing ransom but lacking DR) led to months of degraded services. Good RESPOND capability means you have options beyond paying or suffering.
Respond.key_categories: RS.MA (Incident Management), RS.AN (Incident Analysis), RS.CO (Incident Response Reporting)
Respond.grc_tip: Tabletop exercise reports, post-mortem documents, and IR plan revision history are RESPOND evidence. This IR Center generates all of them.

Recover.purpose: Restore capabilities and services impaired by cybersecurity incidents.
Recover.real_world: DR test results are your RECOVER evidence. Maersk's 10-day recovery from NotPetya shows what happens when RECOVER isn't practiced. Organizations that test DR quarterly discover problems (corrupted backups, missing procedures) before real incidents expose them.
Recover.key_categories: RC.RP (Incident Recovery Plan Execution), RC.CO (Recovery Communication)
Recover.grc_tip: Your DR simulation history IS the evidence for RC.RP. Each test with a pass/fail result, RTO/RPO comparison, and recommendations is exactly what auditors want to see.

compliance_tracking.title: Building a Compliance Tracking Pipeline
compliance_tracking.content: Think of compliance tracking like a CI/CD pipeline for security. Just as DevOps automates code testing and deployment, compliance engineering automates evidence collection and control validation.

pipeline_stages.description: Map framework requirements (NIST CSF, ISO 27001) to specific organizational controls. Each control has: owner, evidence source, test frequency, and acceptance criteria.
pipeline_stages.tool_mapping: The NIST CSF and ISO 27001 tabs show your control mapping. Each control is linked to an implementation status and evidence source.

pipeline_stages.description: Automated and manual evidence collection. Automated: scan reports, log exports, dashboard snapshots. Manual: policy reviews, risk assessments, training records.
pipeline_stages.tool_mapping: Exports from Dashboard (PPTX/JSON), Risk Register (JSON/CSV), and Compliance (JSON) are evidence artifacts. The audit log captures manual activities.

pipeline_stages.description: Validate that controls are operating effectively. This can be automated (continuous monitoring) or manual (quarterly reviews, penetration tests).
pipeline_stages.tool_mapping: DR simulations test recovery controls. Tabletop exercises test IR controls. Architecture assessments test supply chain controls. All logged.

pipeline_stages.description: Identify where controls are missing, partially implemented, or not evidenced. Gaps become risk register items.
pipeline_stages.tool_mapping: The Compliance tab shows Implemented/Partial/Gap status. Each 'Gap' or 'Partial' should have a corresponding risk register entry with a remediation timeline.

pipeline_stages.description: Generate compliance reports for auditors, regulators, and leadership. Include evidence artifacts, control test results, and gap remediation plans.
pipeline_stages.tool_mapping: The Audit Readiness Score, PPTX executive brief, and compliance exports serve this function. The score formula (40% NIST + 35% ISO + 25% Policy) gives a single number that tracks progress over time.

audit_readiness.title: What Auditors Actually Want to See
audit_readiness.content: Auditors don't want to see perfection — they want to see a **defensible program**. That means: risks are identified and tracked, controls are implemented and tested, evidence is organized and accessible, gaps have remediation plans with timelines, and management is engaged (governance).

auditor_questions.question: How do you identify and prioritize cybersecurity risks?
auditor_questions.evidence: Risk register with scoring methodology, heat map, regular review cadence (audit log shows updates)

auditor_questions.question: Show me your incident response capability.
auditor_questions.evidence: IR plan document, tabletop exercise reports (from Tab 2), post-mortem documents (from Tab 3), IR plan revision history

auditor_questions.question: How do you ensure business continuity?
auditor_questions.evidence: DR simulation history with pass/fail results, RTO/RPO comparisons, remediation actions from failed tests

auditor_questions.question: How do you manage third-party risk?
auditor_questions.evidence: Architecture Security Assessment results (Risk Register Tab 2), vendor risk scoring, third-party data sharing agreements

auditor_questions.question: How do you monitor compliance on an ongoing basis?
auditor_questions.evidence: Compliance dashboard with trend data, audit readiness score history, gap analysis with remediation timelines

auditor_questions.question: How do you handle policy management?
auditor_questions.evidence: Policy lifecycle tracker showing current/review/draft/expired status, policy approval dates, review frequencies

navigating_charts.title: Navigating the Compliance Charts & Diagrams
navigating_charts.content: This page contains several interactive visualisations. Each chart tells a different compliance story — here's how to read and navigate them effectively.

charts.name: NIST CSF Function Bar Chart
charts.description: A colour-coded bar chart showing the compliance percentage for each of the six NIST CSF 2.0 functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover.
charts.how_to_read: Each bar represents one CSF function. The height shows the compliance percentage (0–100%). A gold dashed line marks the 80% target — bars falling below this line indicate functions that need attention. Bars are coloured by function (purple = Govern, blue = Identify, green = Protect, orange = Detect, red = Respond, gold = Recover).
charts.what_to_look_for: Functions below the 80% target line are priority remediation areas. Compare relative heights to identify your strongest and weakest functions. Hover over a bar to see the exact percentage.

charts.name: Control Status Legend
charts.description: A visual key showing the seven lifecycle states a control can be in, from fully Implemented (100%) down to Gap (0%).
charts.how_to_read: Each status has an icon, colour, and weight percentage. The weight determines how much credit that status receives toward the compliance score. Implemented = 100%, Compensating = 85%, Largely = 70%, Partial = 40%, Planned = 10%, Not Implemented/Gap = 0%.
charts.what_to_look_for: Use this legend when reviewing the Category-Level Detail expanders below. Controls in 'Compensating' status receive 85% credit but should be verified with your auditor. 'Planned' controls receive only 10% — they barely contribute until work begins.

charts.name: Category-Level Detail (Expanders)
charts.description: Expandable sections for each CSF function showing individual control status, effective weight, evidence freshness, and advisory notes about dependency caps or compensating controls.
charts.how_to_read: Click any function name to expand it. Each control row shows three columns: the control ID and name, its lifecycle status (colour-coded), and its effective weight after penalties. Below each control, captions flag stale evidence (🗂️), dependency caps (🔗), or compensating control notes (🔄).
charts.what_to_look_for: Look for red 'stale evidence' flags — these controls are capped at 50% until evidence is refreshed. Dependency cap notes (🔗) mean a prerequisite control isn't met, limiting this control's score. Address prerequisites first to unlock dependent controls.

charts.name: NIST CSF Sunburst View
charts.description: An interactive hierarchical donut chart. The centre represents the entire NIST CSF framework. The middle ring shows the six functions. The outer ring shows individual controls within each function.
charts.how_to_read: Click on a function segment (middle ring) to zoom into that function and see its controls. Click the centre to zoom back out. Colours reflect the effective weight after evidence staleness and dependency penalties: green (≥80%), light green (60–79%), yellow (40–59%), orange (10–39%), red (<10%). This is not the raw status colour — it shows the actual audit-ready score.
charts.what_to_look_for: Red outer segments are your highest-priority gaps. A function with mostly green segments is healthy. If a function's middle ring is green but outer segments are yellow/red, some controls are dragging the average down — expand the Category Detail above to investigate.

charts.name: ISO 27001 Stacked Bar Chart
charts.description: A stacked bar chart showing control implementation status across ISO 27001 Annex A domains. Each bar is divided into Implemented (green), Partial (yellow), and Gap (red) segments.
charts.how_to_read: Each bar represents one ISO domain. The total bar height shows the total number of controls in that domain. Green = fully implemented, yellow = partially implemented, red = not implemented (gap). A domain with a tall red segment has significant control gaps.
charts.what_to_look_for: Domains with large red segments need remediation plans. Click the Domain Detail expanders below the chart to see health status (Compliant/At Risk/Non-Compliant), evidence freshness, and individual control counts. Stale evidence flags mean the domain score is penalised to 80%.

charts.name: Policy Lifecycle Donut Chart
charts.description: A donut (ring) chart showing the distribution of security policies by status: Current (green), Under Review (yellow), Draft (blue), and Expired (red).
charts.how_to_read: Each segment represents the count of policies in that status. A healthy organisation has mostly green (Current). Any red (Expired) segment is a compliance risk — expired policies are non-compliant. The hole in the centre is decorative; focus on segment proportions.
charts.what_to_look_for: Expired policies require immediate review and re-approval. Policies marked 'Under Review' should have active reviewers assigned. Use the multiselect filter below to focus on specific statuses. Policies expiring within 30 days are flagged with 🔴 EXPIRING SOON.

charts.name: Audit Readiness Gauge
charts.description: A semi-circular gauge showing the composite audit readiness score (0–100%), calculated from NIST CSF (40%), ISO 27001 (35%), and Policy Currency (25%), with a deduction for stale evidence.
charts.how_to_read: The gauge has three colour zones: red (0–40%) = significant gaps, amber (40–70%) = moderate readiness, green (70–100%) = strong readiness. A red threshold line at 70% marks the minimum acceptable score. The breakdown table below the gauge shows how each component contributes to the final score.
charts.what_to_look_for: If the score is below 70%, review the breakdown table to identify which component (NIST, ISO, or Policy) is dragging the score down. The evidence penalty row shows how many points are lost due to stale evidence — refreshing evidence is often the quickest way to improve the score.

overview.title: What is MITRE ATT&CK?
overview.content: MITRE ATT&CK (Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge) is a globally-accessible knowledge base of adversary behavior. It organizes attack techniques into **14 Tactics** (the 'why') and hundreds of **Techniques** (the 'how'). Security teams use ATT&CK to evaluate detection coverage, prioritize defenses, and communicate threats in a common language.

heatmap_guide.title: Reading a Coverage Heatmap
heatmap_guide.content: This heatmap shows how many techniques your organization can detect within each ATT&CK tactic. Green = strong coverage, red = gaps. No organization achieves 100% — the goal is to prioritize detection for techniques used by your most likely threat actors.

attack_chain_guide.title: Understanding Attack Chains
attack_chain_guide.content: An attack chain (or kill chain) models the sequence of tactics an adversary follows during an intrusion. By mapping real-world attacks to ATT&CK tactics, defenders can identify which phases they can detect and where blind spots exist. This is the foundation of **threat-informed defense**.

gap_analysis_guide.title: Using ATT&CK for Gap Analysis
gap_analysis_guide.content: Walk through each tactic and its techniques. Techniques marked as 'Gap' are your detection blind spots — these should be prioritized in your security roadmap. Cross-reference with your NIST CSF Protect and Detect functions for a unified view of control effectiveness.

cases.name: Volt Typhoon — Critical Infrastructure Targeting (2023-2024)
cases.summary: A Chinese state-sponsored group (Volt Typhoon) targeted US critical infrastructure — including water utilities, energy, and transportation — using 'living-off-the-land' techniques. They avoided malware, instead using legitimate tools like PowerShell, WMI, and ntdsutil to blend into normal operations. They maintained persistence for years before detection.
cases.lesson: Traditional signature-based detection misses living-off-the-land attacks. ATT&CK tactics targeted: Initial Access (T1190 — Exploit Public-Facing Application), Persistence (T1078 — Valid Accounts), Lateral Movement (T1021 — Remote Services). Organisations must monitor for anomalous use of legitimate tools, not just malware signatures.
cases.how_it_scores: Techniques used map to ATT&CK T1190, T1078, T1021, T1059 (Command and Scripting Interpreter). If your coverage heat map shows gaps in these techniques, Volt Typhoon-style campaigns would evade your detection.

cases.name: ALPHV/BlackCat — Ransomware-as-a-Service (2023)
cases.summary: ALPHV (BlackCat) ransomware affiliates attacked Change Healthcare, disrupting US pharmacy operations nationwide. The initial access was through compromised credentials (T1078) without MFA. The group exfiltrated 6TB of data before deploying ransomware, leveraging T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) and T1567 (Exfiltration Over Web Service).
cases.lesson: Ransomware groups follow predictable ATT&CK patterns: credential access → lateral movement → data staging → exfiltration → encryption. MFA enforcement blocks the most common initial access vector. Detection at the exfiltration phase (large outbound transfers) provides a last line of defense before encryption.
cases.how_it_scores: The kill chain spans 7+ tactics. Your ATT&CK coverage for T1078 (Valid Accounts), T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact), and T1567 (Exfiltration Over Web Service) directly determines whether you could detect this campaign.

cases.name: CL0P — Supply Chain Mass Exploitation (2023)
cases.summary: CL0P exploited zero-day SQL injection vulnerabilities in MOVEit Transfer (T1190) to steal data from 2,600+ organisations. The attack was automated — no interactive intrusion. The group staged stolen data (T1074) and exfiltrated via web services (T1567), then used extortion without encryption.
cases.lesson: Mass exploitation campaigns target widely-deployed third-party software. ATT&CK coverage of T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1567 (Exfiltration Over Web Service) are essential. Organisations must track which third-party tools are internet-facing and include them in vulnerability management.
cases.how_it_scores: If your gap analysis shows T1190 as a gap, any internet-facing software vulnerability becomes a potential mass exploitation vector. Cross-reference this with your Vendor Risk Registry to identify which vendors operate internet-facing data transfer tools.

threat_actors.title: Key Threat Actor Profiles
threat_actors.content:
Understanding *who* targets your sector is as important as *what* techniques they use. Here are the threat groups most relevant to critical infrastructure and public sector organisations:

**Volt Typhoon (China)** — Targets critical infrastructure for pre-positioning. Uses living-off-the-land techniques exclusively. Focus: Water, energy, transportation. Key ATT&CK: T1190, T1078, T1021, T1059.

**Sandworm (Russia/GRU)** — Destructive operations against critical infrastructure. Known for NotPetya (2017, $10B global damage) and Ukrainian power grid attacks (2015, 2016). Focus: Energy, government. Key ATT&CK: T1059, T1486, T1498 (DoS).

**ALPHV/BlackCat** — Ransomware-as-a-Service operation targeting healthcare, finance, and government. Affiliates use varied initial access, but credential abuse (T1078) is dominant. Changed Healthcare attack disrupted US pharmacy operations.

**CL0P** — Specialises in mass exploitation of file transfer software (Accellion, GoAnywhere, MOVEit). Extortion without encryption. Targets organisations with sensitive data and third-party data processors.

**Lazarus Group (North Korea)** — Financial theft and espionage. Infamous for the Bangladesh Bank heist ($81M, 2016) and cryptocurrency exchange attacks. Key ATT&CK: T1566 (Phishing), T1059, T1005 (Data from Local System).

To use this intelligence: map each actor's known techniques to your coverage heatmap. Gaps in techniques used by actors *targeting your sector* are your highest-priority detection investments.

detection_gaps.title: Prioritising Detection Gaps
detection_gaps.content:
Not all detection gaps are equal. Use this framework to prioritise which techniques to invest in detecting first:

**Tier 1 — Critical Gaps (Immediate Action):** Techniques actively used by threat actors targeting your sector that you currently cannot detect. Example: If Volt Typhoon targets water utilities with T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and your gap analysis shows T1190 as undetected, this is an urgent investment.

**Tier 2 — High-Value Gaps (Next Quarter):** Techniques in the early kill chain (Initial Access, Execution) or late kill chain (Exfiltration, Impact) where detection provides maximum defensive value. Detecting early stops the attack; detecting late prevents the worst outcome.

**Tier 3 — Coverage Broadening (Roadmap):** Techniques in the middle kill chain (Discovery, Collection) where detection adds depth but isn't decisive. These improve investigation capability but are less urgent than Tier 1-2.

**Tip:** Cross-reference this page's gap analysis with the Risk Register. Each undetected technique used by a relevant threat actor should be a scored risk. The risk score then drives CAP creation and resource allocation.

grc_connection.title: GRC Engineering: Threat-Informed Compliance
grc_connection.content:
Traditional compliance asks 'Do you have endpoint protection?' Engineering-driven compliance asks 'Can your endpoint protection detect the techniques used by threat actors targeting your industry?' ATT&CK coverage data is evidence for NIST CSF DE.CM (Continuous Monitoring) and DE.AE (Adverse Event Analysis). The gap analysis feeds directly into your risk register — each undetected technique is a risk that needs scoring and treatment.

This is where GRC transcends checklists. Instead of 'Do you have an IDS? Yes/No,' you can say 'Our detection coverage across ATT&CK is 82%, with gaps prioritized in the risk register and a 6-month roadmap to reach 90%.' That's a fundamentally more mature compliance posture.

navigating_charts.title: Navigating the Threat Intel Charts & Diagrams
navigating_charts.content: The Threat Intelligence page uses stacked bar charts, Sankey diagrams, and detailed technique listings to visualise your MITRE ATT&CK coverage. Here is how to read each one.

charts.name: ATT&CK Coverage Stacked Bar Chart
charts.description: A horizontal stacked bar chart with one bar per ATT&CK tactic. Each bar is split into a green segment (Detected techniques) and a red segment (Gaps).
charts.how_to_read: The total bar length represents the total number of techniques mapped for that tactic. Green = techniques you can detect; Red = blind spots. Bars are sorted by tactic order (Reconnaissance → Impact). A percentage label shows your detection rate per tactic.
charts.what_to_look_for: Tactics with large red segments are your coverage gaps. Prioritise detection for tactics in the early kill chain (Reconnaissance, Initial Access) and high-impact phases (Lateral Movement, Exfiltration). A tactic with 0% detection is a critical blind spot.

charts.name: Sankey Attack Chain Diagram
charts.description: A Sankey (flow) diagram showing how adversary activity flows through ATT&CK tactics from left to right, modelling a realistic multi-stage attack chain.
charts.how_to_read: Nodes on the left are early tactics (Reconnaissance, Initial Access), nodes on the right are late-stage tactics (Exfiltration, Impact). The width of each flow band represents the proportion of techniques connecting those tactics. Node colours correspond to tactic colours used elsewhere on the page.
charts.what_to_look_for: Follow the thickest flows — these are the most common attack progressions. Where a flow passes through a tactic you have low coverage on (from the stacked bar chart), you have a dangerous blind spot in a real attack chain. The goal is to have detection at multiple points along every major flow path.

charts.name: Tactic Coverage Detail Expanders
charts.description: Expandable sections per ATT&CK tactic listing each mapped technique with a Detected (✅) or Gap (❌) status and the associated control.
charts.how_to_read: Click a tactic to expand it. Each row shows the technique ID (e.g., T1566), name, detection status, and which control provides detection. Use the radio filter at the top to show All, Detected Only, or Gaps Only.
charts.what_to_look_for: Focus on Gaps Only view to build a remediation backlog. Techniques with 'recommended' in the control column are aspirational — you haven't deployed detection yet. Cross-reference gap techniques with your risk register to ensure each gap is recorded as a scored risk.

overview.title: Why Audit Logs Matter
overview.content: Audit logs provide a tamper-evident record of who did what, when. They are essential for compliance, incident investigation, and insider threat detection.

deep_dive.title: Audit Logging Best Practices
deep_dive.content: Enterprise audit logging is more than storing events — it's about creating a reliable evidence chain that serves compliance, forensics, and operations.

principles.name: Completeness
principles.definition: Every security-relevant event is captured: user authentication, authorization decisions, data access/modification, configuration changes, privilege escalation.
principles.standard: NIST 800-53 AU-2 lists 25+ event types that should be logged.

principles.name: Integrity
principles.definition: Logs cannot be modified or deleted by the users whose actions they record. Centralize logs to a write-once system (WORM storage) or SIEM.
principles.standard: ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires protection against 'tampering and unauthorized access.'

principles.name: Retention
principles.definition: Logs are retained for a defined period. Regulatory requirements vary: PCI-DSS requires 1 year (3 months immediately accessible), HIPAA requires 6 years, many compliance frameworks suggest 1-7 years.
principles.standard: NIST 800-53 AU-11 addresses audit record retention.

principles.name: Review
principles.definition: Logs are actively reviewed — not just stored. Automated SIEM correlation catches patterns. Manual review catches what automation misses.
principles.standard: ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires logs to be 'regularly reviewed.'

grc_connection.title: GRC Engineering: The Audit Trail as the Foundation
grc_connection.content:
The audit log is the bedrock of compliance engineering. Every action in this platform — risk creation, DR simulation, policy export — is logged with who, what, when. This isn't just good practice, it's the mechanism that makes every other compliance activity auditable. Without audit logs, you have claims. With audit logs, you have evidence.

In a traditional GRC setup, someone manually logs 'Risk reviewed on Q3' in a spreadsheet. In this system, the audit trail automatically records every time a risk is viewed, modified, or reviewed — with the exact user, timestamp, and before/after values. Auditors love this because it's tamper-resistant and complete.

overview.title: What is a Corrective Action Plan?
overview.content:
A Corrective Action Plan (CAP) is a formal remediation commitment raised when a control test fails, an audit finding is issued, or a compliance gap is identified. CAPs are how organisations prove they don't just *find* problems — they *fix* them.

Every CAP has an owner, a priority, a target date, and a defined lifecycle: **Open → In Progress → Pending Verification → Closed**. Closure requires documented resolution notes, ensuring the fix is auditable. ISO 27001 Clause 10.1 requires organisations to 'react to the nonconformity and take action to control and correct it.' CAPs are that mechanism.

lifecycle_states.title: CAP Lifecycle — The Four States
lifecycle_states.content:
Every CAP progresses through four states, each with specific expectations:

**1. Open** — A new finding has been documented. The owner has been assigned, a target date set, and the priority (Critical/High/Medium/Low) reflects business impact. No remediation work has started yet. The clock is ticking toward the target date.

**2. In Progress** — Active remediation is underway. The owner is implementing the fix, patch, or process change. This is where most CAPs should spend the majority of their time. If a CAP stays 'Open' for too long without transitioning, it signals a governance gap.

**3. Pending Verification** — The owner believes the fix is complete and is requesting independent verification. This is a critical quality gate — the person who *fixes* the issue should not be the same person who *verifies* it (segregation of duties). The verifier checks whether the control now passes its test.

**4. Closed** — Verified and documented. Resolution notes are *required* to close a CAP — this is the auditable evidence that the finding was properly addressed. The closed_at timestamp creates a traceable remediation timeline. Auditors look for this exact chain: Finding → Assignment → Remediation → Verification → Closure.

cases.name: Failed WAF Configuration Test
cases.summary: A quarterly control test of PR.PT-4 (Communications and Control Network Protection) revealed the Web Application Firewall was not blocking OWASP Top 10 payloads after a recent rule update. The WAF vendor had pushed a default configuration change that disabled custom rules.
cases.lesson: Control tests must cover post-change validation. The CAP required the infrastructure team to restore custom rules, add a configuration drift alert, and re-test within 14 days. Without the CAP, this regression would have persisted until the next quarterly test — or until an attacker exploited it.
cases.how_it_scores: Priority: Critical. Target date: 14 days. Linked to control PR.PT-4 and risk RISK-017 (Web Application Compromise). Closed in 11 days with resolution notes documenting the rule restoration and drift detection alert.

cases.name: Unpatched VPN Gateway
cases.summary: A vulnerability scan flagged CVE-2024-21762 (Fortinet FortiOS out-of-bounds write) on the organisation's VPN gateway with a CVSS 9.8. The risk register already tracked this as RISK-042 (Unpatched Perimeter Devices), but no remediation action had been taken for 45 days.
cases.lesson: High-scoring risks in the register should automatically generate CAPs when patch windows are missed. The CAP created accountability — the network team had to patch within 7 days, verify the patch, and confirm no IOCs from the exposure window. This closed the loop between risk identification and actual remediation.
cases.how_it_scores: Priority: Critical. Target date: 7 days. Linked to risk RISK-042. Escalated to CISO after 3 days without status update. Closed in 6 days with emergency maintenance window.

cases.name: Missing Disaster Recovery Backup Verification
cases.summary: An audit finding noted that DR backup restoration tests had not been performed for 6 months, violating the organisation's BC/DR policy requiring quarterly tests. The DR Simulator page showed no simulation history for Q1.
cases.lesson: Policy compliance gaps are CAP-worthy findings. The IT Operations team was assigned to run three DR simulations (one per system tier) within 30 days and document recovery times. The CAP linked to control PR.IP-9 (Response Plans) and ensured the audit finding had a traceable remediation path.
cases.how_it_scores: Priority: High. Target date: 30 days. Linked to control PR.IP-9. Closed in 22 days with DR simulation results attached as evidence.

closure_checklist.title: CAP Closure — What Auditors Expect
closure_checklist.content:
Before closing a CAP, verify these five criteria are met:

**1. Resolution Notes Documented** — Describe *what* was done, *when*, and *by whom*. 'Fixed it' is not sufficient. Example: 'Patched FortiOS to 7.4.3 on 2026-03-15 during maintenance window MW-2026-012. Verified no IOCs in 45-day exposure window via SIEM query.'

**2. Independent Verification** — The person closing the CAP should ideally not be the person who performed the fix. This satisfies ISO 27001's segregation of duties requirement.

**3. Root Cause Addressed** — Did the fix address the root cause, or just the symptom? A WAF rule restoration without drift detection will recur. Document preventive measures.

**4. Linked Control Re-Tested** — If the CAP was raised from a failed control test, that control should be re-tested and pass before closure. The test result is your evidence.

**5. Audit Trail Complete** — The CAP's status history (Open → In Progress → Pending Verification → Closed) with timestamps and actors creates the nonconformity management evidence chain that ISO 27001 Clause 10.1 requires.

grc_connection.title: GRC Engineering: CAPs Close the Audit Loop
grc_connection.content:
Finding a problem without fixing it is worse than not finding it at all — it shows you knew and did nothing. CAPs are the bridge between *detection* and *remediation*. In NIST CSF terms, they connect the Detect function (DE.AE — adverse event analysis) to the Respond function (RS.MI — incident mitigation).

In a mature GRC program, every failed control test automatically generates a CAP. The CAP tracks who is responsible, what the target date is, and requires resolution evidence before closure. This creates an unbroken chain: Control Test → Finding → CAP → Remediation → Verification → Closure. Auditors look for exactly this chain when assessing nonconformity management.

try_this.title: Hands-On Exercises
exercises: Raise a new CAP linked to a control ID (e.g., PR.AC-1) and set a target date 30 days out. What priority would you assign a failed access control?
Transition a CAP through all four statuses: Open → In Progress → Pending Verification → Closed. Notice how closure requires resolution notes — this is the evidence an auditor needs.
Filter the CAP register to show only Critical and High priority items. In a real audit, these would be your immediate focus areas.
Find an overdue CAP and examine why it might be stuck. Is the owner assigned? Is the target date realistic? What would you escalate?

overview.title: Why Third-Party Risk Matters
overview.content:
Your security posture is only as strong as your weakest vendor. Third-party risk management (TPRM) ensures that organisations assess, monitor, and govern the security practices of their suppliers, cloud providers, and service partners.

NIST CSF 2.0 added an entire function — **Govern (GV.SC)** — dedicated to Supply Chain Risk Management. ISO 27001:2022 addresses it through A.5.19–A.5.23 (Supplier Relationships). The message is clear: you are accountable for risks introduced by your vendors, even if you don't control their systems.

cases.name: SolarWinds Supply Chain Attack (2020)
cases.summary: Attackers compromised the SolarWinds Orion build pipeline, inserting a backdoor (SUNBURST) into software updates distributed to 18,000+ customers including US government agencies. Victims had no visibility into their vendor's build security.
cases.lesson: Vendor assessments must go beyond questionnaires. Evaluate build pipelines, code signing, and CI/CD security. A single compromised vendor can cascade to thousands of downstream organisations.
cases.how_it_scores: Vendors without verified SDLC controls would score low on the security assessment checklist and be flagged as High or Critical risk tier.

cases.name: MOVEit Transfer Exploit (2023)
cases.summary: The Cl0p ransomware group exploited a zero-day SQL injection in Progress Software's MOVEit Transfer, a managed file transfer tool used by hundreds of organisations. Over 2,600 organisations and 77 million individuals were affected — most were downstream customers of MOVEit users.
cases.lesson: Third-party file transfer tools are high-value targets. Data classification matters — vendors handling Confidential or Restricted data need more frequent assessments and stricter controls.
cases.how_it_scores: A vendor categorised as 'Data Processor' with 'Confidential' data classification would trigger a Critical criticality rating and 180-day reassessment cycle.

cases.name: Okta Support System Breach (2023)
cases.summary: Attackers accessed Okta's customer support case management system using stolen credentials, then used session tokens from uploaded HAR files to impersonate customers. Cloudflare, 1Password, and BeyondTrust were among the downstream victims who detected and blocked the intrusion.
cases.lesson: Even identity providers — your most trusted vendors — can be compromised. Organisations that detected the intrusion had strong monitoring of their own systems. Vendor trust must be verified, not assumed. Include identity providers in your Critical vendor tier with maximum assessment frequency.
cases.how_it_scores: An identity provider handling authentication data would be classified as Critical criticality with Confidential data classification, triggering 180-day assessments and continuous monitoring requirements.

vendor_assessment_guide.title: Vendor Assessment Decision Framework
vendor_assessment_guide.content:
Vendor risk assessment should follow a structured decision tree based on two dimensions: **criticality** and **data classification**.

**Step 1 — Determine Criticality:** How dependent is your organisation on this vendor? Could you operate without them for 24 hours? 7 days? If the answer is 'no' for 24 hours, the vendor is Critical. If 7 days, High. Otherwise, Medium or Low.

**Step 2 — Classify Data Exposure:** What data does the vendor access? Confidential (PII, financial, health) = highest risk. Internal (business processes, operational data) = moderate. Public (marketing, brochures) = lowest.

**Step 3 — Set Assessment Frequency:**
- Critical + Confidential → 180-day cycle, continuous monitoring
- Critical/High + Internal → 365-day cycle
- Medium + any → 18-month cycle
- Low + Public → 24-month cycle

**Step 4 — Define Contractual Controls:** Include security requirements in vendor agreements: right to audit, breach notification SLAs (72 hours for GDPR/PIPEDA), data handling and return/destruction clauses, insurance requirements.

**Step 5 — Monitor Between Assessments:** Watch for: vendor breach news, certificate/domain changes, SOC 2 report expiry, declining assessment scores. Each should trigger an ad-hoc reassessment.

grc_connection.title: GRC Engineering: Vendor Risk in the Framework
grc_connection.content:
NIST CSF 2.0's Govern function includes GV.SC (Supply Chain Risk Management) with categories covering due diligence, contractual requirements, and continuous monitoring. ISO 27001 A.5.19 requires 'processes and procedures for managing information security risks associated with the use of supplier products or services.'

This platform implements tiered vendor risk through criticality-based reassessment intervals: Critical vendors every 180 days, High every 365 days, Medium every 18 months, Low every 2 years. Each assessment uses the same security control checklist and automatically updates the vendor's risk tier. The result is a living vendor risk register — not a one-time spreadsheet that goes stale after procurement.

try_this.title: Hands-On Exercises
exercises: Add a new vendor with 'Critical' criticality and 'Confidential' data classification. Notice how the reassessment interval is set to 180 days automatically.
Run a security assessment on a vendor. Observe how the score determines the risk tier (Critical/High/Medium/Low) and sets the next reassessment date.
Find a vendor with an overdue assessment. What questions would you ask in the reassessment? What evidence would you require?
Cross-reference a Critical vendor with the Threat Intel page — are the ATT&CK techniques used by supply chain attackers (T1195, T1199) covered in your detection?

navigating_charts.title: Navigating the Vendor Registry Charts & Diagrams
navigating_charts.content: The Vendor Registry uses a donut chart and detailed vendor cards to visualise your third-party risk landscape. Here is how to read each one.

charts.name: Vendor Risk Tier Distribution (Donut Chart)
charts.description: A donut (ring) chart showing what proportion of your vendors fall into each risk tier: Critical, High, Medium, Low, and Not Assessed.
charts.how_to_read: Each coloured segment represents one risk tier. The segment size is proportional to the number of vendors in that tier. Hover over a segment to see the exact count. The hole in the centre keeps the chart uncluttered — focus on the relative sizes of segments.
